north_star_verdict.headline: BP Castrol has sat in Qualified for 8+ months with no buyer-facing deliverable since the December 2025 Control Tower demo; the value workshop that would unlock it is deferred to September on travel constraints tied to the regional war, leaving only a narrow window for validation, proposal, and contracting before the 31 December 2026 close.
north_star_verdict.math: Created 14 October 2025, moved Initial Interest to Qualified 31 October 2025, now roughly 265 days old and 178 days from the 31 December 2026 close. Time in Qualified stage is roughly 248 days with no stage progression since the October 2025 RFI submission. The only buyer-facing event was the Control Tower demo on 8 December 2025 (not recorded, roughly 7 months ago). CloseDate was pushed from 14 October 2026 to 31 December 2026 on 4 June 2026 and Amount doubled from 150k to 300k on 30 January 2026. LastActivityDate 23 June 2026 is a rep-side call attempt, not a buyer deliverable. To stay credible for a year-end close the on-site value workshop needs to land and produce a quantified business case by roughly mid-September, then feed proposal and contracting; on current pace that sequence is tight but not yet impossible.
evidence: Value session on-site (Dubai) deferred to September due to travel constraints tied to the regional war; buyer flagged flight risk on the 18 June 2026 rep call attempt.
RFI submitted October 2025, next-step date still pending, no stage movement since.
Control Tower demo 8 December 2025 was the last buyer-facing event and was not recorded, so no transcript exists for this deal.
CloseDate pushed from 14 October 2026 to 31 December 2026 on 4 June 2026 (by Claire Hudson).
LastActivityDate 23 June 2026 is a rep call attempt (rep-side touch), not a completed buyer deliverable.

metrics.narrative: No business metric, cost baseline, savings target, or cycle-time impact has been articulated by BP on any recorded evidence for this deal. The product scope (iSource for eSourcing plus ANA for tail-spend analytics on Castrol) implies the classic tail-spend problem, low-value high-volume indirect purchases that leak savings and evade governance, but the Control Tower demo on 8 December 2025 was not recorded and no Avoma call is associated with this opportunity, so nothing is buyer-quoted. The Next Step names a 'value session' intended to quantify the case, but it is deferred to September 2026 and not yet held. The single highest-leverage move remains landing that workshop and getting the Castrol procurement or finance owner to put a number on the tail-spend leakage.
sources: SF Next_Step__c: 'Wanta value session onsite in dubai'
SF opp name: BP Castrol _Tailspend Ana/eSourcing

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is mapped on the buyer side. There are zero OpportunityContactRole records on this deal and no call in which a BP budget owner appeared. The Next Step line 'Anthony at executive level 12/16/25' refers to Anthony Gray, the DEAL OWNER'S MANAGER on the Zycus side, not a BP executive, so it evidences our own exec reach-in rather than buyer budget authority. What the evidence does show is that BP treats this as a strategic account with multi-region pull, which implies a business-unit or category budget owner for Castrol tail-spend exists but is unidentified. Identifying and getting into a room with that person is a ranked move and a precondition for a credible proposal.
sources: SF Next_Step__c: 'Anthony at executive level 12/16/25' (Anthony Gray = Zycus owner's manager)
SF Next_Step__c: 'Strategic account with multi-region pull'

decision_criteria.narrative: BP's evaluation criteria are not documented in any field and no recorded call captures them. The October 2025 RFI submission means BP has issued formal requirements, so scored criteria exist inside BP's process, but Zycus does not hold a copy of the shortlist scoring or the must-have list. This is a knowledge gap, not merely a hygiene gap: without the RFI criteria we cannot tailor the September value workshop to what BP actually weights. The move is to ask the procurement contact for the RFI evaluation criteria and where Zycus currently stands.
sources: SF Next_Step__c: 'RFI submitted Oct - Pending Sid date'

decision_process.narrative: The buying process is only partially visible. BP ran an RFI in October 2025 and held a multi-stakeholder Control Tower demo on 8 December 2025 (7 BP participants per the prior record), which signals a committee-style evaluation, but the approval chain, the RFI-to-shortlist gate, and who signs off are all unknown. No approval-process, mandate, or decision-process field is populated. The value workshop deferred to September is the next process gate the deal team has visibility into. Mapping the steps from RFI outcome through business-unit approval is required before a year-end close can be forecast with any confidence.
sources: Prior record: Control Tower demo 8 December 2025, 7 BP participants

paper_process.narrative: No contracting mechanics are visible. There is no evidence of MSA, SOW, procurement channel, InfoSec, or legal engagement in any field or activity, which is consistent with an early Qualified-stage deal that has not yet reached commercials. As a new-business enterprise deal into BP, an eventual close would require Zycus SaaS paper (MSA, Order Form, and a separately signed SOW) plus BP's supplier-onboarding and InfoSec review, none of which has started. What would create visibility is the value workshop converting to a commercial conversation; until then paper process is genuinely unknown, not stalled.

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is inferable and prioritized but not buyer-quoted. The opportunity name and product scope (iSource plus ANA for Castrol tail-spend) point to a specific operational pain: poor visibility and control over Castrol's long tail of low-value indirect spend and inefficient sourcing that prevents capturing savings and managing supplier risk. BP validated the pain enough to submit an RFI in October 2025 and convene a 7-person Control Tower demo in December 2025, so it is real and sponsored. What is missing is a verbatim pain statement, a quantified impact, and a named pain owner, because the demo was not recorded and no call is associated with this deal. Getting the Castrol procurement lead or business-unit finance sponsor on record in the September workshop is the move.
sources: SF opp name: BP Castrol _Tailspend Ana/eSourcing
Prior record: Control Tower demo 8 December 2025

champion.narrative: Rebecca Macoustra (Procurement Manager) is the developing champion carried in the deal's living memory: she attended the Control Tower demo on 8 December 2025 (attendee rebecca.macoustra@uk.bp.com) and appeared in recent task contacts, indicating sustained engagement across a multi-month window and facilitation of the 7-person demo. She is not, however, confirmed as an active advocate: there is no recorded call proving she is selling Zycus internally, no evidence of Economic Buyer or Decision Maker access, and no contact role on file this sweep. The fact that the deal has held in Qualified for 8+ months while the value workshop stays unscheduled suggests she may lack the authority or internal air-cover to force progress. 'Developing' is the honest read; the move is to validate whether she can bring the budget owner into the September session or whether we need a second, more senior thread.
sources: Prior canonical record (Control Tower demo 8 December 2025 attendee rebecca.macoustra@uk.bp.com; recent task contacts)

competition.narrative: The competitive set is unknown. No competitor is named in any Salesforce field and no recorded call exists for this deal, so there is no direct evidence of who else BP is evaluating. The October 2025 RFI strongly implies a multi-vendor field (an enterprise of BP's scale and procurement maturity rarely sole-sources a 300k SaaS engagement), but whether an incumbent platform is being displaced or which peers are shortlisted is not visible. This is a material blind spot: without the shortlist we cannot position Zycus differentiation in the September workshop and risk being outflanked by a rival who has mapped BP's criteria. The move is to ask the procurement contact who else is in the RFI shortlist and BP's scoring, before the workshop.
sources: SF Next_Step__c: 'RFI submitted Oct' (implies multi-vendor evaluation)

deal_movement.summary: The pulse is rep-side and cooling: the last two touches (18 and 23 June 2026) are rep call attempts, not buyer deliverables, and both record the buyer as a flight risk unable to travel due to the regional war, with the on-site value session pushed to September. Structurally the deal grew on paper this year (Amount 150k to 300k on 30 January 2026) and the close was pushed out (14 October 2026 to 31 December 2026 on 4 June 2026), but no buyer-facing milestone has landed since the 8 December 2025 Control Tower demo.

competitive_position.summary: Competition is unknown: no competitor is named in Salesforce and no recorded call exists for this deal, so we have no transcript of BP naming alternatives. The October 2025 RFI strongly implies a multi-vendor evaluation, but we do not know who else is shortlisted, whether an incumbent system is being displaced, or what BP's scoring favors. The single strongest CURRENT threat is the unknown peer finalist(s) in the RFI shortlist, because they have equal access to BP's process and we have zero visibility into their positioning. The move is to surface the RFI shortlist and scoring before the September value workshop.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: BP's stated evaluation criteria are not documented (RFI issued October 2025, contents not held by the deal team), so fit against their must-haves cannot be assessed. Product scope (iSource plus ANA) aligns to the stated tail-spend analytics and eSourcing need, but this is inferred alignment, not confirmed against BP's requirements.

best_practice_check.summary: Three levers matter most on this early, single-threaded, war-delayed deal: break the 8-month Qualified stall with a firmly dated workshop, get visibility into the RFI competitive set, and widen beyond one procurement contact into the budget owner.
flags: Convert the deferred value session into a firm, dated on-site workshop with the Castrol budget owner in the room to break the 8-month Qualified stall.
Surface the October 2025 RFI shortlist and BP's scoring so we position Zycus differentiation against the real competitive set, not a blind field.
Multi-thread beyond Rebecca Macoustra into the Castrol business-unit sponsor who owns the tail-spend P&L; today the deal rests on a single developing champion.

items.title: Procurement Manager
items.sentiment: Developing champion: attended the Control Tower demo 8 December 2025 and appears in recent task contacts, but no confirmed advocacy, no Economic Buyer or Decision Maker access, and no contact role on file this sweep.
items.risk: At risk: engagement is real but power access is unconfirmed; if she cannot lock the September workshop or bring the budget owner into it, the deal stays stalled.

champion_strength.summary: Developing champion: Rebecca Macoustra (Procurement Manager) has sustained engagement (December 2025 demo attendance plus recent task contact) and facilitated multi-threading into the 7-person Control Tower demo, but her advocacy, Economic Buyer access, and ability to drive the deal forward are unconfirmed. Eight months in Qualified with the value workshop still unscheduled suggests she may lack the authority or internal sponsorship to move procurement to business-unit sign-off. The read is developing, not strong, and the risk is real: if she cannot unblock the September workshop or bring the pain/budget owner into it, the deal slips past year-end.
champion_strength.source: Prior canonical record (Control Tower demo 8 December 2025 attendee; recent task contacts)

ai_positioning_strength.summary: The AI story is not landing because there is no live engagement to carry it: AIS is Negative (2.0) driven by the absence of calls, tasks, and contact roles, not by a stated buyer rejection of AI. The Merlin/agentic tail-spend narrative has never been put in front of BP on record. When the September workshop lands, leading with Merlin analytics on the Castrol tail is the differentiation play.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS_Score 2.0, AIS_Status Negative. The negative signal reflects an evidence vacuum (no Avoma calls, zero tasks/events/contact roles giving an AI read) rather than a buyer who resisted AI. Treat AI appetite as unknown, not hostile, until the value workshop generates a real signal.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is thinning: the last two touches are rep call attempts, the buyer is a flight risk on the war, and the one substantive event was a December 2025 demo. Not dead (buyer agreed in principle to a September session), but cooling and rep-driven.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: BP Castrol is a strategic-account tail-spend play that has stalled in Qualified for 8+ months; the one thing between us and momentum is landing the war-delayed September value workshop with a quantified case and the Castrol budget owner in the room, against an RFI field we cannot yet see.

win_position.text: This sits low because after 8+ months in Qualified there is still no mapped economic buyer, no quantified business case, and no visibility into the October 2025 RFI shortlist, so BP's actual lean is unknown.
The competitive set is a blind spot: the RFI implies a multi-vendor field yet no rival is named anywhere, so a peer could be positioning against us unseen (source: RFI submitted October 2025).
The deal rests on one developing procurement champion, Rebecca Macoustra, whose authority and budget-owner access are unconfirmed (source: prior record, Control Tower demo 8 December 2025).
BP is genuinely investing: they ran an RFI and convened a 7-person Control Tower demo, and the account grew on paper from 150k to 300k in January 2026, so the interest is real if latent.

deal_momentum.text: No buyer-facing deliverable has landed since the 8 December 2025 demo, and the value workshop is deferred to September on war-related travel constraints (source: Task 18 June 2026).
The last two touches, 18 and 23 June 2026, are rep call attempts with no buyer response logged, so current motion is rep-side, not buyer-pull.
A peace agreement signed in May 2026 may pull the deferred session forward, a potential unblock the deal owner should test now (source: Task 18 June 2026).

customer_commitment.text: BP has made no dated commitment: the September session is a Zycus-proposed placeholder, not a buyer-owned date, and no RFI-outcome date has been shared (source: Next Step, 'Pending Sid date').
No economic buyer or decision maker has been introduced, and there are zero contact roles on the opportunity, so commitment beyond a single procurement contact is unproven.
The buyer did engage a 7-person team for the December 2025 demo, showing the account will convene stakeholders when a session is on the table.

deal_risk.text: Timeline risk dominates: a year-end close needs the September workshop, a quantified proposal, and contracting to run back-to-back, with no slack if travel slips again.
Single-thread and unmapped power: the whole deal depends on one developing champion with no confirmed line to the Castrol budget owner.
Competitive blindness: an active RFI field we cannot see means we could be losing ground without a signal (source: RFI submitted October 2025).

business_case.evidence: No ROI, savings, or quantified tail-spend impact articulated by BP; the value session meant to build it is deferred to September 2026 and not yet held.
